coredata.dc:title: Adsorption and detection tramadol in aqueous solution by fluorescent- magnetic molecularly imprinting polymers

Amini-Fazl, Mohammad sadegh
Mohammadi, Reza
coredata.dc:description:
Background The usage of tramadol (TR) as one of the most common analgesics is expansively growing in the world. Therefore, there is an urgent requirement for a rapid and reliable method for the on-site detection of tramadol in different samples. The elimination half-life of tramadol is about 6 h, and its removal from body is relatively slow. So, it is very critical providing a method for rapid detection of tramadol.
Methods In this study, three magnetic nanocomposites (Fe3O4, Fe3O4/SiO2, and Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC) and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) based on those in the presence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized and investigated for selective adsorption and detection of TR in aqueous solution. For the characterization of synthesized materials, XRD, FT-IR, SEM, VSM, and the response surface method (RSM) were also employed to optimize and estimate the effective parameters of TR adsorption by these materials.
Significant findings The results of investigating the absorption ability of nanocomposites showed that the Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC composite has higher absorption ability than the other two nanocomposites. Moreover, Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC has an excellent ability to adsorb of TR with a removal percentage of 97.46 % at pH = 7, initial TR concentration = 15 mg/L, composite dosage = 1.5 g/L, and contact time = 6 min. According to the experimental results, the imprinting factors (IF) of MIP-Fe3O4, MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2, and MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC are 1.02, 1.72, and 2.19, respectively. Therefore, the MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC was chosen as a selective sensor, and the sensitivity of its response was investigated. The detection of TR was performed by fluorescence spectroscopy at a concentration range of TR of 0 to 250 µM, and a sensitivity of 3.13 a.u./µM and limit of detection of 2.08 µM were determined. Also, the river water and artificial urine samples (with TR of 10 µM) was detected using MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC, and the results of the sensor showed a TR concentration of river water is 9.97 µM and artificial urine is 10.456 µM and which is very good and acceptable precision.

head.ce:title: Adsorption and detection tramadol in aqueous solution by fluorescent- magnetic molecularly imprinting polymers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The usage of tramadol (TR) as one of the most common analgesics is expansively growing in the world. Therefore, there is an urgent requirement for a rapid and reliable method for the on-site detection of tramadol in different samples. The elimination half-life of tramadol is about 6 h, and its removal from body is relatively slow. So, it is very critical providing a method for rapid detection of tramadol.

In this study, three magnetic nanocomposites (Fe3O4, Fe3O4/SiO2, and Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC) and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) based on those in the presence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized and investigated for selective adsorption and detection of TR in aqueous solution. For the characterization of synthesized materials, XRD, FT-IR, SEM, VSM, and the response surface method (RSM) were also employed to optimize and estimate the effective parameters of TR adsorption by these materials.

The results of investigating the absorption ability of nanocomposites showed that the Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC composite has higher absorption ability than the other two nanocomposites. Moreover, Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC has an excellent ability to adsorb of TR with a removal percentage of 97.46 % at pH = 7, initial TR concentration = 15 mg/L, composite dosage = 1.5 g/L, and contact time = 6 min. According to the experimental results, the imprinting factors (IF) of MIP-Fe3O4, MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2, and MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC are 1.02, 1.72, and 2.19, respectively. Therefore, the MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC was chosen as a selective sensor, and the sensitivity of its response was investigated. The detection of TR was performed by fluorescence spectroscopy at a concentration range of TR of 0 to 250 µM, and a sensitivity of 3.13 a.u./µM and limit of detection of 2.08 µM were determined. Also, the river water and artificial urine samples (with TR of 10 µM) was detected using MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC, and the results of the sensor showed a TR concentration of river water is 9.97 µM and artificial urine is 10.456 µM and which is very good and acceptable precision.

ce:section.ce:para: Tramadol [1-(e)-(m-methoxyphenyl)−2-(e)-(dimethylaminomethyl)-cyclohexane-1-(a)-ol] (TR) is a centrally acting analgesic and a member of the opioid family that is widely used for pain treatment in clinical patient care [1–3]. The immediate opioid analgesic effects of tramadol are mediated through its main metabolite O-desmethyltramadol (ODMT) [4]. Therefore, the prescription dosage of tramadol for each patient is directly related to the rate of this metabolism. However, the rate of this metabolism is very individual and not the same for all people. As a result, the reactions to a particular dose of TR might vary from no impact to major adverse effects from overdosing, making the proper administration of TR in clinical patient care incredibly difficult [1]. Therefore, monitoring tramadol levels in the blood and urine during the treatment period is critical for determining the appropriate dose of TR [5].

Various methods were used for the detection of TR in urine, human serum, water, plasma, and blood samples. In a study, capillary electrophoresis with UV laser-induced native fluorescence detection was used as a selective and sensitive method for the determination of TR in the range of 20 ng/L to 5 mg/L [3]. Also, capillary electrophoresis with the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) method was used to detect TR and lidocaine in urine. The method exhibited a linear range for tramadol and lidocaine from 1.0 × 10−7 to 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L with a correlation efficiency of 0.998 [2]. In general, tramadol in various samples was determined using the capillary electrophoresis method in various formats under very different experimental conditions [6]. In a study, for the selective electrochemical determination of TR and ODMT, a tetrahedral amorphous carbon electrode coated with a thin dip-coated recast Nafion membrane was developed. The results of this study showed the limits of detection were 131 nM for TR and 209 nM for ODMT [1]. A sensitive and simple method for thamsylate, tramadol, and lidocaine detection in human urine was developed based on CE coupled with ECL. Results of this study showed that this method is appropriate for the detection of these materials but has disadvantages, including the need for pre-concentrations of analytes [7]. The surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) method was used for the detection of tramadol in water and artificial urine. The results of this study showed that the limit of detection of this method was equal to 5 × 10−5 M [8]. In a study, an electrospun carbon nanofibers-modified screen printed electrode (CNFs/SPE) was used for the rapid, sensitive, and selective determination of tramadol. The limits of detection and quantification for this method are equal to 0.016 nM and 0.05 nM, respectively [9]. The surface of the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was modified by Co3O4 nanoparticles and was used for the detection of tramadol. Also, the electrochemical voltammetry method was used as a detector in this study. By this method, the determination of tramadol was obtained with a linear range of 0.5–45 µM and a detection limit of 0.001 µM [10]. Molecularly imprinted polymers were used for the pre-concentration of tramadol in water, urine, and human serum. Then a high-performance chemo-luminescence method was used as a detector for tramadol. For this method, the best linear calibration graph was obtained in the concentration range of 0.0030–2.5 µM tramadol, with a detection limit of 0.80 nM [11].

As can be seen, various methods have been used for detection of TR in different media, but until now, a portable sensor for fast detection of TR has not been built. Recent years MIP nanoparticle sensors are very promising to become portable devices for detection different materials. Molecularly imprinted polymer due to having specific adsorption sites that are perfectly matched in functional groups, shape, and size with template molecule has been attractive [12–15]. In addition, molecularly imprinted polymers have excellent properties such as easy production, high selectivity and good stability [16,17]. In addition, recent progress in the covalent linkage of MIPs to optical transducers has allowed for the realisation of highly efficient and robust optical MIP-based molecular recognition sensors [18,19]. The MIP binding efficiency, recognition processes and selectivity have been intensively studied by optical means due to the general high sensitivity and simplicity of the utilization of optical techniques [20,21]. The performance of molecularly imprinted polymers is strongly influenced by the number of binding sites, the availability of these sites, the complete washing and remove of the template molecule, which is limited in traditional molecularly imprinted polymers [22,23]. To overcome these limitations and improve the surface of molecularly imprinted polymers, various nanoparticles and nanocomposites such as AgNPs@ZnMOF [24], Clay/GO/Fe3O4 [25], GO/Fe3O4 [26], NiFe2O4@Gr [27] and etc., have been used in the synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers. Because nanostructures have small dimensions and a large surface-to-volume ratio, they can be used to produce more bonding sites and increase the polymer adsorption capacity [28,29]. The use of silica for the modification of QDs sensors has received considerable attention in the last years due to the numerous benefits that silica brings to these composites. In this sense, silica is transparent in the UV–vis spectrum, protects QDs against photochemical degradation and preserves the optical properties of QDs [30]. Despite the mentioned advantages of the use of silica for coating QDs, there are also drawbacks such as the limited porosity of the imprinted layer and the scarce modifiable chemical functionalities. However, organic polymers possess a larger choice of functional groups due to the wide functional monomers and cross-linkers that are commercially available. Thus, the hybrid inorganic-organic MIP-QDs sensors could combine the benefits that silica gives to the optical properties of the QDs, while the recognition of target molecules is performed by the versatile organic imprinted polymer [31]. Furthermore, modifying the MIP polymer with magnetic nanoparticles (in format of core shell) is an interesting way of simplifying its usage [32]. Also, cellulose, one of the most abundant natural polymers, was adopted as a membrane material for optical resolution [33]. Therefore, in this study, an optical method based on molecularly imprinted polymers for easily and effectively detecting TR in water was developed based on Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC. Also, the effect of each component of the Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC nanocomposite on the performance of the sensor was investigated. In addition, three nanocomposites (Fe3O4, Fe3O4/SiO2, Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC) were investigated as adsorbents for TR in water and river water. The results of this study can be used in clinical centers for the detection of TR. Also, the results of this study can be used for wastewater and river treatment.

In this study, iron (II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), ammonia, ethanol, chloroform, methacrylic acid (MAA), azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN), nitric acid, ninhydrin, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), tetrahydrofuran (THF), (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), acrylic acid (AA), carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), and acetic acid were purchased from Merck Co. and without any purification. Ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) was purchased from Sigma Aldridge (Germany) and used without any additional purification. Tramadol hydrochloride (TR) was prepared with a purity of over 90 % by the Pharmaceutical Co. (Iran).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was used to characterize the materials synthesized. The FT-IR spectra of materials were obtained using a Bruker Vector 22 spectrometer at a range of 400–4000 cm−1. The crystal structures of nanocomposites were investigated using the X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyzer Philips PW1730 model. The magnetic properties of the nanocomposites were investigated using the vibrating sample magnetometer method (VSM) and the MPMS-5 SQUID device at room temperature. The morphological and surface characterizations of synthesized materials were investigated by an FE-SEM microscope (SEM- TESCAN MIRA3- FEG).

Graphene quantum dots were prepared by the bottom-up method by pyrolysis of citric acid. First, 2 g of citric acid monohydrate was placed in the oven at 200 °C for 45 min to melt completely. The resulting solution was then added dropwise to 100 ml of NaOH solution at a concentration of 10 mg/ml. After 10 min, the resulting mixture was neutralized using a NaOH solution (0.1 M). The solvents in GQDs were removed by a rotary evaporator, and then GQDs were dried by a freeze dryer [34].

In the first step of the synthesis of MIP-Fe3O4, Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared by a co-precipitation method based on sohrabi et al., method [35] (see Electronic Supporting Material (ESM)). Then, MIP- Fe3O4 was synthesized in three steps. In the first step, 0.10 g of the TR as a template and 0.07 ml of methacrylic acid as a monomer were added to 30 ml of ethanol, and then it was stirred for three hours at room temperature. In the second step, 200 mg of Fe3O4 nanoparticles were added to the above solution and stirred for 3 h. In the third step, 800 µL of EGDMA, 8 mg of AIBN, and 0.10 g of GQDs were added to the solution and degassed for 10 min. Then the reaction was stirred for 12 h at 60 °C and at the end, the synthesized polymer was separated from the solution and dried [36,37]. In order to show that bulk adsorption on polymeric surfaces was not the reason for the tramadol retention by MIP- Fe3O4, non-imprinted polymer NIP-Fe3O4 was also made using the same procedure, but without TR (template).

In first step, Fe3O4/SiO2 nanocomposite was synthesized by Nawaz et al. and Kamari et al. method [38,39] (see ESM). In order to carry out surface polymerization on this nanocomposite, the presence of the vinyl group was necessary; for this purpose, first the amine group was created on its surface [40]. Then Fe3O4/SiO2 -NH2 was synthesized; to perform this reaction, 1 g of Fe3O4/SiO2 nanoparticles was added and dispersed in 20 ml of THF. Then, 0.5 mL of APTES was added to the reaction and stirred for 24 h in reflux conditions and under argon gas at 78–80 °C. The Fe3O4/SiO2 -NH2 was separated from the reaction, and washed by THF and then dried [41]. Amine groups on the surface of Fe3O4/SiO2 -NH2 were converted to vinyl groups in the presence of acrylic acid (Fe3O4/SiO2-NH2-AA) [42]. Then, MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2 was synthesized exactly like MIP- Fe3O4 with the difference that Fe3O4/SiO2-NH2-AA was used instead of Fe3O4. The non-imprinted polymer NIP-Fe3O4/SiO2 was also synthesized using the same procedure as MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2, but without TR (template).

For the synthesis of MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC, in the first step, Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC nanocomposite was synthesized using Fe3O4/SiO2. For this purpose, 0.5 g of Fe3O4/SiO2 in 10 ml of APTMS dispersed and stirred for 2 h (Solution 1). Then, 100 cc of acetic acid and distilled water (1:5) prepared and 1 g CMC was dissolved in it (Solution 2). In the next step, solution 1 was added to solution 2 and the reaction was stirred at 50 °C for 6 h, the reaction was then removed and washed, and the Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC nanocomposite was dried [43]. Then, MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC was synthesized exactly like MIP-Fe3O4 with the difference that Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC was used instead of Fe3O4. The non-imprinted polymer NIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC was also synthesized using the same procedure as MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC, but without TR (template).

MIP-Fe3O4, MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2, and MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC were synthesized as sensor materials for TR detection in aqueous solutions. For this purpose, Fe3O4 nanoparticles, Fe3O4/SiO2 nanocomposite, and Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC nanocomposite as adsorbents for TR were investigated, and the optimum conditions for high percentage adsorption were found by the response surface method (RSM). Then, in optimum conditions, MIP-Fe3O4, MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2, and MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC were investigated. Also, the selectivity and sensitivity of these three MIPs were studied.

For the adsorption of TR from an aqueous solution, different parameters such as pH, time, dosage of adsorbents, and concentration of TR have important effects. In this study, these parameters were studied by RSM and central composite design (CCD). In addition the effects of adsorbents were investigated in this design as a category at three levels (Table 1 ). Based on this design, 84 runs (3 × (2 k + 2k + 4) = 84) were suggested for the study of the adsorption process by Fe3O4, Fe3O4/SiO2, and Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC as adsorbents.

In all of the adsorption experiments concentration of TR in solutions after adsorption were obtained by UV–vis spectrophotometer at λ=272 nm. The adsorption percentage and adsorption capacity were calculated by Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively.

Where, R is adsorption percentage, qe is the adsorption capacity (mg/g), Ce and Ci are the initial and equilibrium concentration of TR (mg/L), respectively, V is the liquid volume (L) and m is the amount of adsorbent was used (g).

To investigate the selectivity of the prepared polymer, some of the substances commonly found with TR in the biological fluids and pharmaceutical samples and often interfere in the determination of TR through conventional methods, such as ascorbic acid, uric acid and citric acid were chosen as interfering species with 10-fold excess of aforementioned substances [44]. For this purpose, binary solutions of TR/ascorbic acid, TR/uric acid, TR/citric acid were prepared with the same concentrations of them. The adsorption experiments in the optimal conditions were performed for three solutions. Then, the concentrations of four molecules remaining in solutions were determined by spectrophotometry. The distribution coefficients of TR, ascorbic acid, uric acid and citric acid were calculated using Equation (3).

In addition, to evaluate the binding site and imprinting factor of the molecularly imprinted polymer synthesized, binding tests and subsequent Scatchard analysis were performed. TR binding tests were performed by MIP and NIP nanocomposites under optimal conditions and in the concentration range of 10 ppm to 50 ppm. For all experiments, the final and initial concentration of TR was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer and the adsorption capacity of each polymer for TR were calculated, then the Scatchard equation (Equation (6)) was used:

Where q is the binding/adsorption capacity of MIP and NIP nanocomposite, Ce is the equilibrium concentration, KD is the association constant, and qmax apparent capacity. The values of qmax and KD are obtained from the intercept and slope of the line drawn in a plot of q/Ce versus q [45,46]. Then, the imprinting factor (IF) is calculated using the following equation [47]:

To evaluate the fluorescence performance, several solutions were prepared of TR in concentration range of 0 to 250 µM (10 ml) and optimal pH. Then 0.001 g of MIPs was dispersed (5 min) in all of them. The fluorescence intensity of each sample with an excitation wavelength of 430 nm and slit width of 0.5 nm was obtained with a fluorescence spectrophotometer at 350 nm–550 nm wavelengths. In addition, all these experiments were performed with three replications. Then, the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) were calculated from the calibration line at low concentrations by Eqs. (8) and (9) [48,49].
(8)

In these equations, Sa and b are the standard deviation of the intercept and the slope of the regression line, respectively. The response equation and linear model (approximately) of the sensor were also obtained based on the results of this section. Then, the sensitivity of the sensor was determined from the slope of the approximately linear model. In order to check the ability of the sensor in real river water samples with the addition of a specific amount of tramadol, it was investigated. First, 2.63×10−3 g of tramadol was added to one liter of river water and artificial urine (concentration of 10 µM), then the amount of tramadol was measured using this sensor. Then, the amount of sensor response and 10 µM were compared.

Scheme 1 shows the synthesis method of polymers that are produced in this study. First, Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized by co-precipitation method then were divided into two parts. First part was used for synthesize MIP-Fe3O4 by EGDMA and MAA for TR. Then the SiO2 nanoparticles were synthesized in surface of second part of Fe3O4 nanoparticles with O-H groups on Fe3O4 surface. Then NH2 groups were created on the surface of Fe3O4/SiO2 nanoparticles using APTES. Fe3O4/SiO2-NH2 nanoparticles were divided into two parts. First part was used for synthesize Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC nanocomposite and the NH2 groups on the surface of Fe3O4/SiO2-NH2 nanoparticles were converted to vinyl using acrylic acid, and MIP was synthesized on that surface for TR. In final step, in the surface of Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC nanocomposite MIP was synthesized for TR by EGDMA and MAA. In MIPs polymers the non-covalent interaction between the template molecule (TR) and the functional groups in the structure of the monomer (MAA), and the result is the creation of suitable active sites for the template molecule.

Fig. 1 shows the FT-IR spectra of all nanoparticle, nanocomposites and polymers. As can be seen in spectrum of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (curve a) an absorption bands at 565 cm−1 exist which show the vibration of Fe-O [50]. In addition, the peaks at 1599 cm−1 and 3418 cm−1 corresponding to the vibration of hydroxil groups (-OH) on the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [51]. In FT-IR spectra of the other materials, the absorption intensity of Fe–O group decreases with the addition of other compounds [52]. The spectrum of Fe3O4/SiO2 (curve b) showed the strong peaks at 1087 cm−1 and 802 cm−1 which related to the un-symmetric and symmetric linear stretching vibrations of Si-O-Si bonding, respectively. The bending vibration absorption peak of Si-O-Si was observed at 466 cm−1. Another characteristic signal 1087 cm−1 was attributable to asymmetric Si-O-Si vibration or Si-O-Fe stretching vibration in the silica shell [53]. In the spectrum of Fe3O4/SiO2 -NH2 (curve c), the stretching and bending vibrations of amino groups were detected at 1542 cm−1, 1461 cm−1 and 733 cm−1, which suggested that amino groups have been anchored on the surface of silica coating. Successful functionalization of amino propyl on silica coating was further confirmed by the presence of C-H stretching vibration observed at 2864 cm−1 and 2920 cm−1 [53,54]. Ninhydrin reacts with the α-amino group of primary amino acids producing ‘Ruhemann's purple’. The chromophore formed is the same for all primary amino acids. The intensity of the color formed depends on the number and chemical nature of the amino groups being analysed. Ruhemann's purple has a spectral maximum at 570 nm (Fig. 1(j)) [55]. As shown in Fig. 1(d), a peak at approximately 1670 cm−1 observed that is related to C=C stretching which confirmed that the surface of Fe3O4/SiO2-NH2-AA was successfully grafted by double bond [56]. FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC was shown in Fig. 1(e), as can be seen, stretching vibrations of C=O bond of carboxylate groups and bending vibrations of CH2 appeared at 1576 cm−1 and 1442 cm−1, respectively. Peaks at 1086–1184 cm−1 are related to C–O bond vibrations [43]. Also, a sharp absorption band located at 2120 cm−1 appeared which was attributed to the CN stretching vibration [57]. Fig. 1(f) is the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs, there is a broad peak at 3453 cm−1 is related to –OH and –NH groups stretching vibration. Also, the well-defined peak at 1585 cm−1 is assigned to bending vibrations of C=C group. The peaks at 1389 cm−1, and 1077 cm−1 were related to the C−O (carboxy), and C−O (alkoxy) functional groups, respectively [58]. In FT-IR spectra of all three molecular imprinted polymers (Fig. 1 (g–i)), compared to their nanocomposites, a peck at 1730 cm−1 approximately has been created, which can indicate the presence of EGDMA in the polymers structures [59,60]. Also, in the spectra of three molecular imprinted polymers the characteristic peaks of GQDs are observed. In addition in all MIPs the peak of O-H vibration (in 3400 cm−1) were shifted, which can illustrate the interaction of tramadol molecules with MAA via hydrogen Bond.

In Fig. 2 showed the XRD results for Fe3O4 nanoparticle, Fe3O4/SiO2, Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC, GQDs, MIP- Fe3O4, MIP- Fe3O4/SiO2, MIP- Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC. As can be seen, all of these materials, except GQDs, have crystalline and semi-crystalline structure and GQDs has an amorphous structure with a broad peak. In the spectrum of Fe3O4 nanoparticle six pecks can be observed in the range of 2θ (˚) equal to 30.48˚, 35.82̊, 43.56̊, 53.94̊, 57.4̊, and 63.04̊ were observed showing that Fe3O4 nanoparticles have been successfully synthesized and have face centered cubic (FCC) structure [61,62]. According to the Fig. 2-b, the position of the peaks in the XRD spectrum of Fe3O4 nanoparticles did not change after coating by SiO2, which indicates the stability of the crystalline phase of these nanoparticles. However, based on XRD results, the crystalline size of the synthesized Fe3O4 nanoparticles and Fe3O4/SiO2 were 35.4 nm and 19.3 nm, respectively. In Fig. 2-c, the XRD spectrum of Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC can be seen, in which, in addition to the peaks of iron nanoparticles, a broad peak can be seen in 20˚, which can be caused by CMC [63]. The XRD spectrum of GQDs was shown in Fig. 2-d, and a broad peak can be seen in 35˚, which can also be seen in the structure of polymers (Fig. 2 e–g) [64]. As can be seen, the peaks of the basic nanoparticles and nanocomposites can be seen in the spectrum related to polymers, and the peak related to GQDs is also weakly visible. These results indicate the successful synthesis of the desired materials.

The SEM images of the synthesized materials and their particle size distribution were shown in Fig. 3 . As can be seen, Fe3O4 nanoparticles have a spherical structure with an average diameter of approximately 46 nm (Fig. 3-a). After SiO2 nanoparticles are placed on its surface, it is still a spherical structure, but its average diameter has increased to approximately 75 nm (Fig. 3-b). CMC is a natural polymer that has an irregular structure, and the same can be seen in the SEM results of the Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC nanocomposite (Fig. 3-c). As can be seen, Fe3O4/SiO2 was placed on the CMC surface and its average diameter has increased to approximately 101 nm (Fig. 3-c). After the formation of a molecular imprinted polymer layer on the surface of these materials, their morphology has not changed because the polymerization is surfaced (Fig. 3 d–f), but their average diameter has increased greatly (Fig. 3 d–f). Also, non-imprinted polymers (NIP) are like MIPs, but they have a more irregular shape and are aggregated (Fig. 3 g–i), so their diameter is larger than MIP (Fig. 3 g–i). In addition, the mapping an analysis was done for MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC and result of them was shown in Fig. S1 (see ESM file). Elemental mapping analysis shows that elements are present evenly throughout the polymer which reconfirms the presence and uniform distribution of nanoparticles.

The results of VSM analysis were shown in Fig. 4 , as can be seen, all materials are without magnetic hysteresis (there is no loop diagram.). The VSM diagram of Fe3O4 nanoparticles is the highest and its magnetic saturation value is equal to 84.35 emu.g−1. After placing SiO2 nanoparticles on the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, its magnetic saturation value decreased to 47.70 emu.g−1, which is due to the non-magnetic structure of SiO2. Also, the addition of CMC has also reduced the magnetic saturation to 24.25 emu.g−1. In addition, magnetic saturation of MIP-Fe3O4, MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2, and MIP- Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC were equal to 23.14 emu.g−1, 8.34 emu.g−1, and 3.45 emu.g−1, respectively. Also, according to the S-shaped diagrams of the materials and the magnetic saturation value, it can be concluded that the materials have paramagnetic properties and can be easily separated from aqueous solutions by using an external magnetic field.

In this study for investigation of optimize condition response surface methodology and central composite design (in four factors and three categories: adsorbents) were used. Results of ANOVA analysis was reported in Table 2 , as can be seen, the model F-value of 302.68 implies the model is significant. There is only a 0.01 % chance that an F-value this large could occur due to noise. P-values less than 0.0500 indicate model terms are significant. In this case X1 (pH), X2 (time), X3 (concentration), X4 (dosage), X5 (categories: adsorbent), X2×3, X4 X5, X1², X2², X3², X4² are significant model terms. The Lack of Fit F-value of 0.03 implies the Lack of Fit is not significant relative to the pure error. There is a 100.00 % chance that a “Lack of Fit F-value” this large could occur due to noise. The predicted R² of 0.9779 is in reasonable agreement with the adjusted R² of 0.9793; i.e. the difference is less than 0.2. Adequate precision measures the signal to noise ratio. A ratio greater than 4 is desirable. This ratio of 66.046 indicates an adequate signal. Therefore, the prediction model (Eq. (10)) can be used to navigate the design space.

A= −117.15 for Fe3O4, −104.24 for Fe3O4/SiO2 and −76.66 for Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC adsorbent.

The equation in terms of actual factors can be used to make predictions about the response for given levels of each factor. Here, the levels should be specified in the original units for each factor. In Fig. 5 , perturbation graphs of all adsorbents and that's average was shown. As can be seen, the adsorption percentage was increased by increasing the dosage and time and reducing concentration in pH=7, for all adsorbents. In addition Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC adsorbent has higher adsorption percentage to compare of the other adsorbents. The optimal conditions was shown in Fig. 6, in this profile can be seen Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC is best adsorbent. In this condition the adsorption process was done and results of them showed adsorption percentage is equal to%97.46. Therefore Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC composite was choosing for basic of target MIP. But, for addition investigation, Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/SiO2 was experimented as basic for MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The results of selectivity of MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: All experimental in this section were done in optimal condition that is determined in the 3.2 section. The results of selectivity for binary solution were given in Table 3 . For three binary solutions, k and kˊ for MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC were higher than others. The high values of k and kˊ mean that MIP- Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC acts selectively for tramadol. Also, the values related to the imprinting factors (IF) of all polymers are given in Table 4 . The imprinting factors of MIP-Fe3O4, MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2, MIP- Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC are 1.02, 1.72, and 2.19, respectively. Thus, MIP-Fe3O4 and MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2 not have well IF values and their selectivity are weak to tramadol, which can be seen in the values in Table 3. Therefore, the sensitivity and reusability study just accrued for MIP- Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC.

For investigation of reusability and stability for MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC, in first step the best pH for desorption was study. Results of this study showed in Fig. 7 -a, based on this results best pH is 1. Thus, adsorption process in optimal condition was performed and desorption was performed in pH= 1. The results showed that the adsorption percentage of TR by MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC after 15 cycles is approximately 80 % (Fig. 7-b). Therefore this polymer is stable and suitable and can be used in industrial and commercial works. In addition, in the optimum condition preconcentration factor for MIP- Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC was calculated as the ratio of TR concentrations after adsorption (2 mL) to concentrations before desorption (500 µL). The results of them showed the preconcentration factor for MIP- Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC was 3.97.

To evaluate the detection performance of the proposed sensor, specific dosages of the MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC were separately dispersed in different concentrations of TR. Then the fluorescence emission spectra of these samples were obtained under the optimal experimental conditions. For the evaluation of the sensor's detection sensitivity, the correlation of fluorescence intensity with TR concentration was conducted, and the results are provided in Fig. 8 . As can be seen, the fluorescence signal gradually increase as the TR concentration is raised from 0 to 250 µM (Fig. 8 -a). The fluorescence intensity was increased via π-π interaction, and exhibits stable solid-state photo-luminescence (PL) to monitor the drug loading and releasing through the Förster resonance energy transfer with the TR like doxorubicin [65,66]. The fluorescence intensity of these samples at 430 nm is represented in Fig. 8 -b. The linear model of these data was calculated by Excel software (Eq. (11)). As can be seen, the R2 and sensitivity are equal to 0.9988 and 3.13 a.u./µM, respectively. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity shows an excellent linear relationship with the TR concentration.
(11)
y = 3.1287 x + 127.91

The LOD and LOQ of this sensor were calculated by the calibration line at low concentrations. The results show that the LOD and LOQ are equal to 2.08 µM and 6.3 µM of TR concentration, respectively. The LOD of HPLC for TR detection was reported is equal to 5.16 µM (1.36 µg/mL) [67]. Therefore the LOD of MIP- Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC is better than the HPLC method. The real sample of river waters (with tramadol in concentration of 10 µM) was detection by MIP- Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC. The sensor response for this sample is shown in Fig. 8 -c. As can be seen, the obtained peak intensity is equal to159.11 a.u, which using the sensor response equation, the detected concentration value is equal to 9.972 µM. Also, the artificial urine (complex solution of Ascorbic Acid, Uric Acid, and Citric Acid) with tramadol in concentration of 10 µM was detection by MIP- Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC. The sensor response for this sample is shown in Fig. 8-d. As can be seen, the obtained peak intensity is equal to160.62 a.u, which using the sensor response equation, the detected concentration value is equal to 10.456 µM. Therefore, the sensor material synthesized and produced in this study (MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC) is capable of detecting tramadol in disturbed environments with acceptable precision. Reproducibility of MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC sensor up to 5 cycles for 10 µM concentration of TR was studied and result was shown in Fig. 8 -e. In addition HPLC-UV method (Agilent Technology 1260 infinity) was used for TR detection in river water and artificial urine (with tramadol in concentration of 10 µM). The results of HPLC method showed the concentration of TR in river water and artificial urine are 10.74 µM and 10.98 µM, respectively.

In this study, three magnetic nanocomposites include Fe3O4, Fe3O4/SiO2, and Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC were synthesized and investigated for adsorption tramadol in aqueous solutions. Then, molecularly imprinted polymers based on these nanocomposites in presence of GQDs were synthesized for TR as template molecule. The XRD, FT-IR, SEM, VSM analysis were used for characterization of all materials. The ability of MIPs as optical chemosensors was investigated. In the first step, the optimal conditions for the adsorption of TR by these nanocomposites were studied by response surface methodology (RSM). The results showed the best conditions for maximum adsorption are pH = 7, initial TR concentration = 15 mg/L, dosage = 1.5 g/L, contact time = 6 min, and adsorbent is Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC. Also, in these conditions, the adsorption percentage is 97.46 %. Results of selectivity investigations shows the IF of MIP-Fe3O4, MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2, and MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC are 1.02, 1.72, and 2.19, respectively. Therefore, the MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC was chosen as a selective sensor and the sensitivity of its response was investigated. The detection of TR was performed by fluorescence spectroscopy at a concentration range of 0 to 250 µM, and a sensitivity of 3.13 a.u./µM and a LOD and LOQ of 2.08 µM and 6.3 µM, respectively, were determined. Also, the river water and artificial urine samples, which had a tramadol concentration of 10 µM, were detection using MIP-Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC, and the results of the sensor showed concentration of these samples is 9.97 µM and 10.456 µM, respectively. Generally, according to the results of this study, the MIP- Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC is the best chemosensor material for the detection of TR in an aqueous solution. Also, the MIP- Fe3O4/SiO2/CMC polymer is prepared from materials at a reasonable price, and considering its reusability up to 15 cycles, this can be an economical method.
